Scenario: You receive an email from 'gtbank-security@gtb-alerts.net' claiming your GTBank account has been debited ₦85,000 without authorisation. A button says 'DISPUTE THIS TRANSACTION NOW' and links to 'gtbank-secure-dispute.com'.
OptionA: Click the dispute button immediately to recover your money
OptionB: Forward the email to GTBank's official fraud line at gtbankfraud@gtbank.com
OptionC: Open your GTBank app directly and check your transaction history yourself
CorrectAnswer: Open your GTBank app directly and check your transaction history yourself
Hint: GTBank's real domain is gtbank.com — the sender domain 'gtb-alerts.net' is a fraudulent lookalike. Always verify unexpected debit alerts directly in your banking app, never through email links.
Explanation: This phishing email weaponises Fear of financial loss. The fraudulent domain 'gtb-alerts.net' mimics GTBank branding. Clicking the link leads to a credential-harvesting page. Forwarding to 'gtbankfraud@gtbank.com' is also risky — verify the email address is exactly correct. Your banking app is always the safest first check.

Scenario: An email arrives with GTBank letterhead: 'Your BVN verification has expired. Under CBN Circular BPS/DIR/GEN/LAB/07/025, all customers must re-verify their BVN by clicking below within 48 hours or face account restriction.' The sender is 'cbn-bvn@nigeria-banking-alert.com'.
OptionA: Re-verify your BVN via the link — CBN regulations are mandatory
OptionB: Call GTBank customer care on 0700-482-6726 (from the back of your card) to confirm this requirement
OptionC: Reply to the email asking for the specific CBN circular reference
CorrectAnswer: Call GTBank customer care on 0700-482-6726 (from the back of your card) to confirm this requirement
Hint: The CBN never communicates directly with individual customers via email. Any regulatory compliance for BVN is communicated by your bank through official channels — not from a domain like 'nigeria-banking-alert.com'.
Explanation: This phishing attack cites a real-sounding regulatory reference (fake) to create False Legitimacy and Official Authority pressure. The 48-hour deadline adds Urgency. Replying to the email exposes you to further social engineering. The CBN-mandated BVN process is managed by your bank — not via unsolicited email links.

Scenario: You receive a professionally designed email from 'support@accessbank-onlinebanking.ng' with your first name in the greeting: 'Dear [Your Name], unusual login detected from Kano, Nigeria at 03:14AM. If this was not you, verify your identity at the link below within 15 minutes or your account will be locked for 72 hours for investigation.'
OptionA: Click the link immediately — 15 minutes is very short and 72-hour lock is serious
OptionB: Open a new browser tab, type accessbankplc.com yourself, and check your account security settings
OptionC: Reply to the email confirming it was not you so they stop the investigation
CorrectAnswer: Open a new browser tab, type accessbankplc.com yourself, and check your account security settings
Hint: The domain 'accessbank-onlinebanking.ng' is NOT Access Bank's real domain (accessbankplc.com). The personalised first name came from a prior data breach — attackers use it to add False Legitimacy. The 15-minute timer is an extreme Urgency tactic designed to bypass rational thinking.
Explanation: This is a spear-phishing attack using personalisation from breached data to create False Familiarity. The ultra-short 15-minute window is the most extreme Urgency tactic, designed to make you act before you think. Replying confirms your email address as active. Always type your bank's URL directly — never follow email links to banking pages.

Scenario: You receive an email: 'Dear Student, your university bursary payment of ₦45,000 has been processed. To receive your funds, log in to verify your UBA account details at: uba-student-bursary.com within 24 hours.'
OptionA: Click the link and enter your UBA details to collect your bursary
OptionB: Check with your bursary office in person or via your official student portal
OptionC: Forward the link to your coursemates who may also be eligible
CorrectAnswer: Check with your bursary office in person or via your official student portal
Hint: UBA's official domain is ubagroup.com — 'uba-student-bursary.com' is a fake domain designed to harvest your banking credentials. University bursary disbursements are announced through official student portals, not unsolicited emails.
Explanation: Students are prime targets for bursary phishing because of financial stress. This combines Greed (unexpected money) and Urgency (24 hours). The lookalike domain steals credentials. Forwarding amplifies the attack to more victims.

Scenario: Your company receives an invoice email from 'finance@firstbank-ng-payments.com': 'Please find attached the July reconciliation statement from First Bank. Kindly review and approve payment of ₦2.1M before COB today.' The attached PDF contains a 'View Statement' button.
OptionA: Open the PDF and click 'View Statement' — reconciliation must not be delayed
OptionB: Call your First Bank relationship manager directly on their known number to verify this invoice
OptionC: Forward to your accounts team to handle
CorrectAnswer: Call your First Bank relationship manager directly on their known number to verify this invoice
Hint: First Bank's domain is firstbanknigeria.com — 'firstbank-ng-payments.com' is fraudulent. The PDF attachment with an embedded 'View Statement' button is a two-layer attack: the PDF itself could be malicious, and the button likely leads to a credential harvest page.
Explanation: This is a Business Email Compromise (BEC) attack targeting corporate finance processes. The dual-layer attack (email + malicious PDF) bypasses single-layer security controls. COB deadline creates Urgency. The IT professional should know that PDFs with embedded external links are a documented malware delivery vector.

Scenario: A caller says: 'Good evening, I'm calling from Zenith Bank Fraud Squad. We have flagged a suspicious transfer of ₦150,000 from your account to an unknown account in Kano. To cancel it before it clears, I need you to confirm your account number and your last 4 ATM PIN digits right now.'
OptionA: Provide the details quickly — ₦150,000 is too much to lose
OptionB: Provide your account number only — not the PIN digits
OptionC: Hang up and call Zenith Bank directly on 0700-050-0000 from their official website
CorrectAnswer: Hang up and call Zenith Bank directly on 0700-050-0000 from their official website
Hint: Zenith Bank will NEVER ask for your PIN digits over the phone — not even the last 4. This is the single clearest red flag of any banking Vishing call. Your PIN is for you alone.
Explanation: This Vishing attack uses Fear (large loss) and creates False Urgency (transfer about to clear). The 'partial PIN' request is a social engineering trap — even last 4 digits narrow down your PIN significantly. Calling back on an independently verified number always exposes the fraud.

Scenario: A call: 'This is the EFCC Cybercrime Division. Your bank account has been identified in a money laundering investigation. You must immediately transfer your funds to a CBN Escrow Account we provide for safekeeping during the investigation period, or your account will be frozen and assets seized.'
OptionA: Transfer to the escrow account to protect your funds from seizure
OptionB: Ask for a case reference number and hang up to verify with EFCC on 09-904-9900
OptionC: Transfer half your funds as a compromise
CorrectAnswer: Ask for a case reference number and hang up to verify with EFCC on 09-904-9900
Hint: No legitimate agency — EFCC, CBN, NDIC, or NFIU — will ever instruct you to transfer your own money to an 'escrow account' by phone. This is the textbook definition of a recovery scam combined with impersonation fraud.
Explanation: This is an Impersonation + Advance Fee Fraud attack. The 'CBN Escrow Account' is the attacker's own account. The threat of seizure creates extreme Fear. The EFCC never contacts individuals this way — all investigation notices are formal, in writing, and served through proper legal channels.

Scenario: A call to your government office line: 'Good morning, I'm from the PENCOM Help Desk. Our records show your RSA pin number has been used to attempt an early withdrawal that you did not authorise. To lock your account immediately, please confirm your RSA PIN and NIN for identity verification.'
OptionA: Confirm the details to protect your pension savings
OptionB: Tell the caller you'll call PENCOM directly on 09-462-8000 and hang up
OptionC: Give only your NIN but not your RSA PIN
CorrectAnswer: Tell the caller you'll call PENCOM directly on 09-462-8000 and hang up
Hint: PENCOM never calls individuals to request RSA PINs or NIN. RSA PINs are private codes that protect your retirement savings — disclosing them is equivalent to handing over your retirement account.
Explanation: Pension fraud targeting government employees is rising. This attack combines Fear (retirement savings at risk) and Authority (PENCOM official). 'Partial data' is not safe — your NIN combined with name/date of birth enables full identity reconstruction. Always call the institution directly using a number from their official website.

Scenario: SMS: 'GTBank ALERT: Your account has been debited ₦25,000. If you did not authorise this transaction, click to reverse: gtbank-reversal-ng.com/reverse?ref=8821. Act within 30 mins.'
OptionA: Click the link immediately to reverse the unauthorised debit
OptionB: Call GTBank on 0700-482-6726 to check your actual balance and transaction history
OptionC: Reply STOP to cancel the debit
CorrectAnswer: Call GTBank on 0700-482-6726 to check your actual balance and transaction history
Hint: GTBank sends genuine transaction alerts from registered short codes, not links to external domains. 'gtbank-reversal-ng.com' is a credential-harvesting domain. Replying 'STOP' confirms your number is active.
Explanation: This Smishing attack exploits Fear of financial loss and creates extreme Urgency (30 minutes). The reference number creates False Legitimacy. Bank debit reversal is done through your banking app or by calling your bank — never through an SMS link to an unknown domain.

Scenario: SMS: 'UBA: Dear customer, your internet banking access will be suspended in 2 hours due to incomplete KYC documentation required by CBN Circular 2025/013. Update at: uba-kyc-update.ng to maintain access.'
OptionA: Update your KYC immediately to avoid losing internet banking access
OptionB: Log into your actual UBA banking app to check if any KYC action is required
OptionC: Visit the link on your laptop where it is safer to review
CorrectAnswer: Log into your actual UBA banking app to check if any KYC action is required
Hint: UBA's official domain is ubagroup.com — 'uba-kyc-update.ng' is fraudulent. Real KYC notifications from UBA appear in your official banking app with specific instructions, not via SMS links.
Explanation: This Smishing attack uses regulatory language (CBN Circular) to create False Authority and Urgency. The .ng TLD gives a false sense of Nigerian legitimacy. 'Visiting on a laptop' doesn't make an attacker-controlled site safer — the credential theft happens regardless of device.

Scenario: A well-dressed person approaches you at a bank ATM: 'Please, I'm in an emergency. My card is stuck and I have a sick child at the hospital. Can you transfer ₦10,000 to my Opay number? I'll use internet banking to refund you immediately once I get inside.' They show you a hospital invoice on their phone.
OptionA: Transfer the money — a sick child is an emergency
OptionB: Politely decline and direct them to speak with the bank's security officers inside
OptionC: Transfer ₦5,000 as a compromise
CorrectAnswer: Politely decline and direct them to speak with the bank's security officers inside
Hint: Physical social engineering at ATMs is a documented attack. The hospital invoice can be a fake screenshot. Bank security officers are the correct resource for genuine banking emergencies.
Explanation: This is a Physical Pretexting attack exploiting Compassion and Urgency. The hospital invoice is a prop to create False Legitimacy. ATM social engineering often involves a second attacker nearby watching you enter your PIN while you're distracted by the conversation.

Scenario: Someone connects with you on LinkedIn claiming to be a Stanbic IBTC Wealth Manager: 'I specialise in helping young Nigerians grow their savings through our premium investment products. I can offer you 25% monthly returns on a ₦500,000 minimum investment. I need just your BVN and bank statement for KYC before onboarding.' They have a verified-looking profile with 800+ connections.
OptionA: Provide the KYC details — 25% monthly return is a life-changing opportunity
OptionB: Research Stanbic IBTC's official investment products at stanbicibtcbank.com before engaging
OptionC: Ask to meet in person at a Stanbic branch before providing any documents
CorrectAnswer: Research Stanbic IBTC's official investment products at stanbicibtcbank.com before engaging
Hint: 25% monthly returns (300% annual) is a Ponzi scheme indicator — no legitimate investment product offers guaranteed returns at this rate. Stanbic IBTC's official contact for wealth management is through their registered branches and official website.
Explanation: LinkedIn investment scams are sophisticated Pretexting attacks. The 800+ connections creates Social Proof. 25% monthly is mathematically impossible from legitimate investments. Your BVN and bank statement together enable identity theft, loan fraud, and account takeover. Even meeting in person can be faked — verify through official Stanbic channels first.

Scenario: You find an envelope on your office printer labelled: 'CONFIDENTIAL — Q3 Salary Adjustment Notice — All Staff'. Inside is a QR code with instructions to scan to view your adjusted salary grade.
OptionA: Scan the QR code — salary information is directly relevant to you
OptionB: Report the found envelope to your IT security desk without scanning the QR code
OptionC: Scan the QR code from your personal phone instead of your work device
CorrectAnswer: Report the found envelope to your IT security desk without scanning the QR code
Hint: QR codes are the modern equivalent of a malicious link. Scanning routes your device browser to an attacker-controlled URL. Using your personal phone doesn't protect you — it just moves the infection target.
Explanation: This is a targeted Baiting attack using Self-Interest (salary information) to compel action. QR code attacks bypass corporate email filters and web proxies. Personal devices are still valuable targets — they often have access to corporate apps and email accounts.

Scenario: You receive a WhatsApp message from an unknown number: 'I found this USB drive in the ministry carpark labelled Ministry Internal Audit 2025. I've uploaded its contents to this Google Drive link for everyone's convenience: [link].' The link appears to be a real Google Drive URL.
OptionA: Open the Google Drive link — the person was thoughtful to share the contents
OptionB: Report the original USB device and this message to your ministry's IT security without clicking any link
OptionC: Download the files quickly before the link expires
CorrectAnswer: Report the original USB device and this message to your ministry's IT security without clicking any link
Hint: The attacker created the WhatsApp message AND the USB device. The 'helpful stranger' narrative is designed to extend reach. Even a real Google Drive URL can host malware-laced documents. The IT security desk can forensically examine both.
Explanation: This is a multi-stage Baiting attack using Social Proof (someone 'helpfully' shared it) and Curiosity. The Google Drive URL is legitimate — attackers use real cloud storage to bypass URL blacklisting. Malicious Office or PDF documents hosted there can execute macros or exploits on open.

Scenario: Your ministry email receives: 'IPPIS URGENT: Salary batch processing error detected on your profile. Log in to correct your account details at ippis-update.gov-ng.com before Friday payroll run. Failure means withheld salary.'
OptionA: Click and update immediately before the payroll deadline
OptionB: Check the official IPPIS self-service portal at ippis.gov.ng directly
OptionC: Reply asking for more information about the error
CorrectAnswer: Check the official IPPIS self-service portal at ippis.gov.ng directly
Hint: The IPPIS official domain is ippis.gov.ng. 'ippis-update.gov-ng.com' is a fraudulent domain — note that '.gov-ng.com' is NOT a government TLD. Nigerian government domains end in .gov.ng.
Explanation: Government payroll phishing exploits every civil servant's fundamental concern — receiving their salary. The fake domain mimics a .gov.ng address but is actually a .com domain. Payroll corrections are made through the official IPPIS self-service portal.

Scenario: You receive an official-looking email: 'FEDERAL CIVIL SERVICE COMMISSION: Annual performance appraisal results are ready. Download your appraisal letter to confirm your step increment at: fsc-appraisal-portal.com. Deadline: end of business today.'
OptionA: Download the appraisal letter immediately — promotion increments matter
OptionB: Check the official FCSC portal at fedcivilservice.gov.ng for any announcements
OptionC: Forward to your colleagues who would also want their appraisals
CorrectAnswer: Check the official FCSC portal at fedcivilservice.gov.ng for any announcements
Hint: FCSC's official domain is fedcivilservice.gov.ng. 'fsc-appraisal-portal.com' is fraudulent. Appraisal communications are formal processes that go through official ministry channels and the official portal.
Explanation: This attack exploits Career Aspiration (promotion increments) and Urgency (end of day deadline). The download action is the attack vector — the 'appraisal letter' is a malicious document. Forwarding to colleagues amplifies the attack.

Scenario: Email from 'jamb-admissions@jamb.edu.ng.admissions-portal.com': 'Your Direct Entry admission has been processed. To download your admission letter, log in at jamb-admission-ng.com with your JAMB registration number and date of birth.'
OptionA: Log in immediately — your admission letter is critical
OptionB: Go directly to jamb.gov.ng in your browser and check your profile
OptionC: Call a fellow student who was also applying to see if they received the same email
CorrectAnswer: Go directly to jamb.gov.ng in your browser and check your profile
Hint: JAMB's domain is jamb.gov.ng — 'jamb.edu.ng.admissions-portal.com' is a lookalike subdomain trick. 'admissions-portal.com' is the actual domain, not JAMB's. Your JAMB registration number and date of birth together are enough for identity theft.
Explanation: This phishing attack exploits one of the most emotionally significant moments in a student's life — university admission. The subdomain trick (jamb.edu.ng.admissions-portal.com) is designed to confuse — only the last two parts before the TLD matter as the domain. Always type jamb.gov.ng directly.

Scenario: SMS: 'NIMC: Your NIN has been deactivated due to suspicious activity. Reactivate within 24hrs at: nimc-reactivate.com.ng or face permanent suspension of NIN-linked services (SIM, BVN, passport).'
OptionA: Reactivate immediately — losing NIN-linked services is catastrophic
OptionB: Visit a NIMC enrolment centre or call 0800-800-0634 to verify
OptionC: Text back to request more information
CorrectAnswer: Visit a NIMC enrolment centre or call 0800-800-0634 to verify
Hint: NIMC does not deactivate NINs via SMS and does not send reactivation links. NIN issues are resolved at official NIMC enrolment centres. The .com.ng TLD does not mean a site is official.
Explanation: This attack creates Fear by threatening cascading consequences (SIM, BVN, passport all linked to NIN). The threat of 'permanent suspension' maximises the fear response. NIMC has never had an SMS-based deactivation process.

Scenario: A call: 'Good afternoon, I'm from the Office of the NSA. We are conducting a classified investigation into financial irregularities at your ministry. Your name appears in our preliminary findings. To be cleared before the formal list is published, you must attend a confidential debriefing at an undisclosed location today. Do not discuss this with your supervisor.'
OptionA: Attend the debriefing — being on an NSA list is extremely serious
OptionB: Refuse, end the call, and report it immediately to your supervisor and ministry security
OptionC: Ask for the caller's name and promise to attend tomorrow
CorrectAnswer: Refuse, end the call, and report it immediately to your supervisor and ministry security
Hint: National security agencies conduct formal investigations through official legal processes. Unsolicited phone calls requesting you to attend 'undisclosed locations' without supervisor knowledge are a textbook kidnapping-risk and social engineering red flag.
Explanation: This is an extreme Vishing attack using a fabricated security threat and Isolation tactics ('don't tell your supervisor'). In Nigeria, this pattern has been used to extort money or enable kidnapping. Any call asking you to meet at an undisclosed location without management knowledge is dangerous.

Scenario: A person visits your home claiming to be from the National Population Commission: 'We are conducting the 2025 National Housing Census. I need your full household roster including names, dates of birth, NINs, and employment details for all residents. This is mandatory under the Statistics Act.'
OptionA: Provide all the information — it is a government-mandated census
OptionB: Ask to see an official ID, verify the ID on the NPC website at population.gov.ng, and confirm before sharing any NIN details
OptionC: Give names and ages but refuse to give NINs
CorrectAnswer: Ask to see an official ID, verify the ID on the NPC website at population.gov.ng, and confirm before sharing any NIN details
Hint: Legitimate NPC enumerators carry verifiable photo IDs with QR codes or reference numbers you can verify on population.gov.ng. NIN numbers are NOT collected in general housing census operations — any enumeration asking for NINs is suspicious.
Explanation: This physical Pretexting attack exploits Civic Compliance (it's the law) and Authority (government official). Real NPC enumerators do not collect NIN numbers. Providing all household NINs enables mass identity theft for every person in your home.
PsychPrinciple: Civic Compliance + Authority + Mass Identity Risk

Scenario: Email: 'UNILAG PORTAL MAINTENANCE: Your student portal account will be deactivated in 48 hours unless you verify at: student-portal-unilag.com. Use your matric number and password.'
OptionA: Verify immediately — losing portal access means missing assignments
OptionB: Type portal.unilag.edu.ng directly in your browser to check your account status
OptionC: Ask your department's IT officer
CorrectAnswer: Type portal.unilag.edu.ng directly in your browser to check your account status
Hint: University portals have official .edu.ng domains. 'student-portal-unilag.com' is fraudulent. Entering your portal credentials on a fake site hands the attacker full access to your academic records, results, and financial information.
Explanation: Student portal phishing targets academic anxiety — fear of missing deadlines or losing access. University IT departments announce maintenance through official portals and notice boards, not email links to external domains.

Scenario: SMS: 'WAEC NOTICE: Due to a system upgrade, your WASSCE result scratch card has been invalidated. Purchase a new card at waec-result-ng.com for ₦3,500. Old cards expire in 24hrs.'
OptionA: Buy the new scratch card quickly before your results are locked
OptionB: Check waec.gov.ng or call WAEC on 01-270-3781 to verify
OptionC: Ask a classmate if they received the same message
CorrectAnswer: Check waec.gov.ng or call WAEC on 01-270-3781 to verify
Hint: WAEC does not invalidate purchased scratch cards and has never communicated result-access changes via SMS. 'waec-result-ng.com' is not WAEC's domain (waec.gov.ng). This is an advance fee fraud targeting exam anxiety.
Explanation: WAEC and NECO result access scams are extremely common. They exploit Educational Anxiety and Urgency. ₦3,500 is within a student's budget, making the ask feel proportionate. WAEC scratch cards, once purchased from authorised vendors, remain valid.
PsychPrinciple: Educational Anxiety + Urgency + Advance Fee Fraud

Scenario: A call: 'Hello, this is the JAMB Central Admission Processing Centre. We have a vacancy in Computer Science at UI. However, there is a processing fee of ₦45,000 required within 2 hours to secure the spot before it goes to the next candidate on the list.'
OptionA: Pay immediately — UI Computer Science is a top programme
OptionB: Tell the caller you'll call back and call JAMB on 09-292-000-1 to verify the offer
OptionC: Negotiate the fee down and then pay
CorrectAnswer: Tell the caller you'll call back and call JAMB on 09-292-000-1 to verify the offer
Hint: JAMB does not offer admission places over the phone with payment requirements. Admission processes are entirely online through the JAMB portal. Any 'pay to secure a spot' call is an advance fee fraud.
Explanation: This Vishing attack exploits the immense pressure of university admission in Nigeria. The 2-hour window is designed to prevent verification. JAMB's entire admission process is transparent and online — no official processing fees are ever collected by phone.
PsychPrinciple: Educational Desperation + Extreme Urgency + Advance Fee Fraud

Scenario: A senior student asks: 'I'm doing a study on student financial habits for my thesis. Can you fill out this quick Google Form? It just asks about your monthly allowance, how you receive it (cash or bank transfer), and which bank you use.' The form also has fields for your account number.
OptionA: Fill it out — helping a senior's thesis is a good thing
OptionB: Fill out only the non-financial questions and skip the account number field
OptionC: Refuse and ask why an account number is needed for a financial habits study
CorrectAnswer: Refuse and ask why an account number is needed for a financial habits study
Hint: Legitimate academic surveys on financial habits do not require real account numbers — researchers use anonymised ranges (e.g. 'above/below ₦30,000'). Combining account number + bank name + your identity enables fraudulent transactions.
Explanation: This Pretexting attack uses Academic Trust and Reciprocity (helping a peer). The Google Form gives it a veneer of legitimacy. Your account number, bank, and identity together are enough to attempt fraudulent transfers or SIM swaps. Legitimate IRB-approved research never collects actual account numbers.

Scenario: An email from 'github-security@github-noreply-secure.com': 'Unusual login detected on your GitHub account from Lagos, Nigeria. Two new deploy keys were added. If this was not you, revoke access immediately at: github-security-review.com.' Your company's codebase is on GitHub.
OptionA: Click immediately to revoke the deploy keys before your codebase is compromised
OptionB: Go directly to github.com in your browser, log in, and check Settings > Security > Active Sessions
OptionC: Forward to your team lead as it involves company code
CorrectAnswer: Go directly to github.com in your browser, log in, and check Settings > Security > Active Sessions
Hint: GitHub's official domain is github.com — 'github-noreply-secure.com' is fraudulent. GitHub security emails come from noreply@github.com. Going directly to github.com lets you see the actual security log.
Explanation: Developer-targeted phishing uses the credible threat of codebase compromise to trigger immediate action. Deploy key theft could give attackers access to your production systems. The 'Lagos, Nigeria' location detail is designed to make the alert feel more real and personal.

Scenario: Email to hospital staff: 'NHIS SYSTEM UPDATE: Healthcare provider credentials must be renewed by end of week. Providers failing to renew will have claims suspended. Complete renewal at: nhis-provider-renewal.com.'
OptionA: Renew immediately to prevent claims suspension
OptionB: Visit nhis.gov.ng directly and check the provider portal for renewal notices
OptionC: Ask your hospital's compliance department to handle it
CorrectAnswer: Visit nhis.gov.ng directly and check the provider portal for renewal notices
Hint: NHIS provider credential renewal is managed through nhis.gov.ng's official provider portal. Any external domain collecting provider credentials is a phishing attempt. Your compliance department verifying through official channels is also appropriate.
Explanation: NHIS phishing targeting healthcare providers exploits their professional obligation to maintain active credentials for patient billing. Claims suspension would affect both the hospital and patients.
PsychPrinciple: Professional Obligation + Fear of Business Disruption

Scenario: SMS: 'MDCN URGENT: Your medical practising licence MDN/2025/XXX is due for renewal. Incomplete renewals after this notice will result in deregistration. Renew at: mdcn-licence-ng.com for ₦25,000.'
OptionA: Pay the renewal fee immediately — deregistration ends your career
OptionB: Check the MDCN website at mdcn.gov.ng or visit a MDCN office to verify renewal requirements
OptionC: Pay ₦25,000 — it's a small fee to avoid deregistration
CorrectAnswer: Check the MDCN website at mdcn.gov.ng or visit a MDCN office to verify renewal requirements
Hint: MDCN's renewal process is conducted through their official portal at mdcn.gov.ng. Licence numbers are not formatted as 'MDN/2025/XXX' — the real format is different. MDCN sends renewal notices through formal written communications, not SMS.
Explanation: Medical licence fraud is particularly effective against healthcare workers because the threat is their entire career and livelihood. MDCN renewal fees and processes are published on their official website and have not changed to an SMS-based system.

Scenario: Call at 10pm: 'Doctor, this is Dr. Ola Bello, CMAC at LUTH. We have a critical situation — a patient from your ward has developed post-operative complications and the duty registrar cannot reach you on the ward number. I need you to authorise an emergency protocol change and provide your hospital EMR login so we can update the patient file remotely before the on-call team arrives.'
OptionA: Provide the EMR login — patient safety is the highest priority
OptionB: Call the LUTH CMAC office directly on the hospital's main number to verify the emergency
OptionC: Ask for the patient's name first before providing credentials
CorrectAnswer: Call the LUTH CMAC office directly on the hospital's main number to verify the emergency
Hint: Healthcare EMR credentials are never shared — not even in genuine emergencies. Hospitals have emergency break-glass procedures that do not require sharing login credentials. Calling the hospital's main number to verify takes 60 seconds.
Explanation: This is the most dangerous type of healthcare social engineering — exploiting a doctor's Duty of Care at the most psychologically vulnerable moment (late night, emergency framing). EMR credential sharing violates data protection law and hospital policy regardless of the apparent emergency. Break-glass accounts exist for exactly this reason.
PsychPrinciple: Duty of Care + Manufactured Emergency + Night-time Vulnerability

Scenario: A man in a suit visits your consulting room claiming to be from the Nigerian Medical Association research department: 'We are auditing prescribing patterns for antibiotics. May I take a photo of your prescription pad and prescriber stamp for our records? It helps us verify the sample size.'
OptionA: Allow the photo — NMA research is legitimate
OptionB: Decline and ask him to contact your hospital administration for any authorised audit
OptionC: Allow the photo but cover your full name
CorrectAnswer: Decline and ask him to contact your hospital administration for any authorised audit
Hint: Prescription pad images and prescriber stamps can be used to forge prescriptions for controlled substances. NMA does not conduct audits this way. Legitimate researchers go through hospital ethics boards.
Explanation: This physical Pretexting attack uses Academic Authority to obtain prescription forgery materials. Prescription pads and stamps are regulated documents. Any unauthorised copying enables controlled substance fraud — a serious criminal offence.

Scenario: SMS: 'NHIS: You have a pending medical benefit claim of ₦180,000. To process your payment, provide your HMO card number and NIN at: nhis-claims-ng.com within 48hrs or forfeit the benefit.'
OptionA: Claim the benefit quickly before it expires
OptionB: Call your HMO's official customer care line to verify any pending claims
OptionC: Visit the website from your laptop for better security
CorrectAnswer: Call your HMO's official customer care line to verify any pending claims
Hint: HMO benefit claims are processed through your employer's HR department or directly at HMO offices — not through SMS links. ₦180,000 is a suspiciously specific amount designed to seem credible.
Explanation: Medical benefit fraud exploits healthcare illiteracy and the appeal of unexpected financial gain. HMO card numbers and NINs together enable identity fraud and fraudulent medical claims in your name.
PsychPrinciple: Greed + False Legitimacy + Medical Identity Theft

Scenario: Company-wide email: 'IT NOTICE: Microsoft 365 licenses are being reallocated. Users who do not confirm activity at office365-confirm-ng.com by EOD will have accounts deactivated to free up licenses.'
OptionA: Confirm immediately — losing email access impacts work
OptionB: Check with your IT department directly before clicking any external link
OptionC: Confirm quickly and inform colleagues
CorrectAnswer: Check with your IT department directly before clicking any external link
Hint: Microsoft 365 license management is done by your IT admin through the real Microsoft 365 admin portal — not by sending users to external domains. IT notices about account changes come from your company's own IT helpdesk.
Explanation: Internal phishing (emails appearing to come from IT) are particularly effective because recipients assume they are legitimate. An IT professional should know that Microsoft 365 management never requires employees to confirm activity on external sites.

Scenario: Call: 'Hi, I'm Dare from Palo Alto Networks technical support. Our monitoring has detected unusual outbound traffic from your company's IP range that matches a known C2 callback. I need to walk you through a quick diagnostic. Can you install this remote monitoring agent: pa-support-tool.exe from our support portal?'
OptionA: Install the agent — C2 callbacks are a serious threat
OptionB: Thank the caller and raise a formal support ticket with Palo Alto Networks through your official account
OptionC: Ask for their employee ID before installing
CorrectAnswer: Thank the caller and raise a formal support ticket with Palo Alto Networks through your official account
Hint: Vendor technical support never makes unsolicited calls requesting remote agent installation. A C2 callback alert from a vendor would come through your registered support account, not a cold call. 'pa-support-tool.exe' from an unknown URL is likely a RAT.
Explanation: This vishing attack targets IT professionals using technical credibility (C2 callbacks are real threats) and vendor impersonation. Unsolicited 'helpful' support calls are a common initial access vector. Always raise support tickets through your registered vendor account portal.

Scenario: A new contractor starts at your company. On day 2, he asks: 'I'm still waiting for my domain account. Can I use your credentials temporarily to access the project repo? I won't change anything — just read-only. The project deadline is tomorrow and my access request is stuck in HR.' He seems genuine and friendly.
OptionA: Share credentials temporarily — it's just read access for one day
OptionB: Escalate his access request through proper channels and offer to retrieve the specific files he needs yourself
OptionC: Create a temporary local account for him
CorrectAnswer: Escalate his access request through proper channels and offer to retrieve the specific files he needs yourself
Hint: Credential sharing violates your company's security policy and your personal accountability regardless of the reason. 'Read-only' access with someone else's credentials is still credential abuse. Escalating his access request takes minimal effort.
Explanation: This is an insider threat / Pretexting scenario using Likeability (friendly new colleague) and a Reasonable Justification (deadline pressure). Shared credentials are unauditable — if anything goes wrong, it appears under your account. Proper access escalation exists precisely for this.
PsychPrinciple: Likeability + Reasonable Justification + Credential Sharing Risk

Scenario: Job application confirmation email: 'Congratulations! You have been shortlisted for the Dangote Group Graduate Trainee Programme 2025. To complete your application, you must pay a ₦15,000 non-refundable assessment fee at: dangote-recruitment-ng.com within 24hrs.'
OptionA: Pay the fee — Dangote Graduate Trainee is a prestigious opportunity
OptionB: Verify the recruitment programme at the official Dangote Group website (dangote.com) or their official social media
OptionC: Pay and ask for a receipt to confirm legitimacy
CorrectAnswer: Verify the recruitment programme at the official Dangote Group website (dangote.com) or their official social media
Hint: Legitimate companies never charge application or assessment fees to candidates. Dangote Group and all reputable Nigerian employers conduct recruitment entirely free of charge. Any fee request is a definitive sign of fraud.
Explanation: Job offer scams are extremely common in Nigeria where unemployment is high. They exploit Career Aspiration and Urgency. 'Non-refundable' is designed to prevent you from thinking about loss. Dangote Group's real recruitment is managed through verified channels.
PsychPrinciple: Career Aspiration + Urgency + Advance Fee Fraud

Scenario: At a tech conference, you collect branded merchandise including a 'USB conference pack' from an unmarked booth. The pack includes a USB drive labelled 'Conference Slides & Speaker Resources 2025'. Back at the office, you want to review the slides.
OptionA: Plug in the USB on your office computer — it's from a conference
OptionB: Plug in the USB in an airgapped analysis VM before opening any files
OptionC: Upload the USB content to VirusTotal first
CorrectAnswer: Plug in the USB in an airgapped analysis VM before opening any files
Hint: Conference USB drops are a documented attack vector specifically targeting IT and security professionals at industry events. VirusTotal won't catch BadUSB attacks (firmware-level). An airgapped VM is the correct forensic approach.
Explanation: Attacking security professionals at conferences via USB drops is documented (Defcon, Black Hat USB drops). BadUSB attacks operate at firmware level before any antivirus can respond. Only hardware-isolated airgapped analysis environments provide proper containment.

Scenario: A Facebook message from what appears to be your friend's account: 'Hey! I just got ₦200,000 from this investment platform, it's legit. Start with just ₦10,000 and get ₦100,000 in 7 days. Here is the link: [link]. I've done it 3 times already.'
OptionA: Try with ₦10,000 — your friend has already profited from it
OptionB: Call your friend on their real phone number to ask if they actually sent this message
OptionC: Try with a smaller amount like ₦5,000 to test it
CorrectAnswer: Call your friend on their real phone number to ask if they actually sent this message
Hint: Facebook accounts are commonly hacked and used to send investment scam messages to all contacts. The 'your friend recommends it' is Social Proof. Your friend likely does not know their account is compromised.
Explanation: This is a compromised social media account attack using Social Proof (trusted friend) and Greed (1000% in 7 days is mathematically impossible from legitimate investments). These are Ponzi/pyramid schemes. Your friend's account was compromised — they need to be informed.

Scenario: Instagram DM: 'Hey babe! I'm verified on TikTok and I'm looking for people to help promote products. I'll pay you ₦50,000 per week just for sharing my content. I need your phone number and bank account to set up payments. Reply now!'
OptionA: Reply and provide the details — ₦50,000 per week for sharing content is easy money
OptionB: Research the account thoroughly and never provide banking details to an unverified stranger
OptionC: Provide your phone number but not your bank account
CorrectAnswer: Research the account thoroughly and never provide banking details to an unverified stranger
Hint: Influencer payment scams on social media collect banking details for money mule schemes. You would receive stolen money in your account and be asked to forward it — making you legally complicit in fraud.
Explanation: Social media money mule recruitment uses Flattery and easy money to target young people. Victims receive stolen funds in their accounts and are instructed to transfer to another account, making them liable for fraud. Your bank account in a money mule scheme can result in a criminal record.

Scenario: You receive a Twitter/X DM from an account with 12,000 followers: 'I'm a cybersecurity researcher. I found a critical RCE vulnerability on your company's login page. To share the full report responsibly, I need you to whitelist my IP and create a test account: researcher@protonmail.com. I'll share the report once I have access.'
OptionA: Create the test account — responsible disclosure is important
OptionB: Direct them to your company's official vulnerability disclosure programme and bug bounty email
OptionC: Ask for the vulnerability details first before creating any account
CorrectAnswer: Direct them to your company's official vulnerability disclosure programme and bug bounty email
Hint: Legitimate security researchers follow coordinated disclosure protocols. They submit findings to official bug bounty programmes and VDP contact addresses — they do not request network access before disclosing vulnerabilities.
Explanation: This is a social engineering attack impersonating a security researcher. Real researchers don't need IP whitelisting or test accounts to demonstrate a vulnerability — a Proof of Concept (PoC) screenshot or video is standard practice. Granting access 'in exchange for the report' is the classic attacker pivot.

Scenario: Email: 'Your Facebook account has been reported for violating Community Standards. It will be permanently disabled in 24 hours. Appeal this decision immediately at: facebook-appeal-support.com to preserve your account.'
OptionA: Appeal immediately — losing a Facebook account means losing contacts and memories
OptionB: Check your actual Facebook notifications by typing facebook.com in your browser
OptionC: Click the link quickly before the 24 hours expire
CorrectAnswer: Check your actual Facebook notifications by typing facebook.com in your browser
Hint: Facebook handles account issues through notifications within the Facebook app and email from @facebookmail.com. 'facebook-appeal-support.com' is a credential harvesting site. All your Facebook account issues are accessible at facebook.com directly.
Explanation: Social media account loss phishing is extremely common. The threat of losing your social network is a powerful Fear trigger. Account appeals are handled entirely within Facebook's official platform — no external website.
PsychPrinciple: Fear of Social Loss + False Urgency

Scenario: WhatsApp message: 'Hi! I'm Fola. I'm studying abroad and my scholarship portal requires 5 Nigerian students to take a 10-minute English language proficiency test to validate our research. ₦5,000 paid per test via Opay. Just follow this link to register with your full name, school email, and NIN.'
OptionA: Register — ₦5,000 for 10 minutes is a good rate and helping research is positive
OptionB: Decline — legitimate paid research studies do not require NIN and are advertised through official institutional channels
OptionC: Take the test but provide a fake NIN
CorrectAnswer: Decline — legitimate paid research studies do not require NIN and are advertised through official institutional channels
Hint: Academic research paid studies are recruited through university ethics board-approved channels and never require NIN. Your NIN combined with name, school email, and WhatsApp number enables comprehensive identity profiling.
Explanation: This Smishing attack uses Academic Credibility, moderate Greed, and Urgency to collect high-value identity data under the guise of research. Legitimate student research pays through institutional systems after proper disclosure. NIN is never required for a language proficiency test.
PsychPrinciple: Academic Credibility + Moderate Greed + Identity Harvesting

Scenario: SMS from 'MTN-NG': '🎉 You've been selected for MTN 1GB free data! Claim before it expires: mtn-promo-claim.com. Tap the link and enter your phone number and MTN PIN.'
OptionA: Claim the free data quickly before it expires
OptionB: Check for legitimate MTN promotions through the MyMTN app or by dialling *131#
OptionC: Enter only your phone number but not your PIN
CorrectAnswer: Check for legitimate MTN promotions through the MyMTN app or by dialling *131#
Hint: MTN distributes free data promotions through the MyMTN app, *131# USSD, and official SMS from registered short codes. Your MTN PIN is your account master key — entering it on any non-MTN site gives the attacker full control of your line.
Explanation: Telecom promotional fraud is endemic. 'Free data' exploits both Greed and the everyday relevance of mobile data. Your MTN PIN can be used to port your number (SIM swap), redirect calls, and access any service linked to your phone number.

Scenario: Call: 'Congratulations! You are today's lucky winner of the Airtel 20th Anniversary Promo — a brand new iPhone 15 and ₦500,000 cash. To claim your prize, you need to pay ₦20,000 processing and delivery tax to an Airtel escrow account. We'll deliver to your home.'
OptionA: Pay the ₦20,000 — ₦500,000 and an iPhone is worth the processing fee
OptionB: Hang up — no legitimate prize requires upfront payment
OptionC: Negotiate the processing fee down before paying
CorrectAnswer: Hang up — no legitimate prize requires upfront payment
Hint: Advance fee fraud (pay to claim a prize) is the oldest form of Nigerian internet scam. Legitimate competition prizes are delivered without any upfront payment. Airtel and all telecom operators do not collect prize fees by phone.
Explanation: The '419' Advance Fee Fraud principle — you pay a small fee to claim a large reward that never arrives. Telecom prize scams work because Airtel, MTN, and Glo do run real promotions, giving the scam a veneer of plausibility.

Scenario: SMS: 'Glo: Dear subscriber, your line is scheduled for deactivation due to inactivity. To reactivate, topup ₦500 using recharge card and send card pin to 0810-xxx-xxxx to confirm your line is active.'
OptionA: Buy a ₦500 recharge card and send the PIN to reactivate
OptionB: Dial *777# or call Glo on 121 to check your account status directly
OptionC: Send ₦200 instead to test if it's legitimate
CorrectAnswer: Dial *777# or call Glo on 121 to check your account status directly
Hint: Telecom reactivation NEVER involves sending recharge card PINs to a phone number. Sending the PIN gives the recipient immediate access to ₦500 of airtime. Glo deactivation is handled through your SIM card's own USSD menu.
Explanation: Recharge card PIN scams are low-tech but effective. The fraud collects small amounts from many victims simultaneously. Glo (and all Nigerian telcos) manage account reactivation through their USSD systems — no PIN sharing is ever required.

Scenario: Email to your company IT team: 'Glo Corporate Account Alert: Your organisation's bulk SMS allowance will expire in 48 hours. Log in to renew at glo-corporate-billing.com with your corporate account credentials before service interruption.'
OptionA: Log in and renew to prevent service disruption
OptionB: Check Glo's official corporate portal or call your account manager directly
OptionC: Ask a colleague to handle it
CorrectAnswer: Check Glo's official corporate portal or call your account manager directly
Hint: Glo's corporate billing is managed through their official corporate portal and dedicated account managers. External billing domains are not used by Nigerian telecom operators for corporate account management.
Explanation: Corporate telecom billing phishing targets IT admins who manage company communications. Credential theft from a corporate telecoms account enables interception of SMS OTPs across the entire organisation.
PsychPrinciple: Professional Responsibility + Urgency + Corporate OTP Risk

Scenario: A visitor to your home claims to be an MTN technician: 'We are upgrading the mobile network in this area and I need to verify your SIM card registration details to ensure uninterrupted service. Please show me your SIM pack and provide your NIN for re-registration.'
OptionA: Cooperate — uninterrupted mobile service is important
OptionB: Tell him to leave and call MTN on 180 to verify if any home visits are being conducted
OptionC: Give him only your NIN but not your SIM pack
CorrectAnswer: Tell him to leave and call MTN on 180 to verify if any home visits are being conducted
Hint: MTN and other Nigerian telecoms do not conduct door-to-door SIM re-registration visits. This is a known SIM swap fraud preparation method — your SIM details and NIN together allow a fraudster to port your number to a new SIM.
Explanation: Physical Pretexting for SIM swap fraud. Your SIM details + NIN enable a full SIM swap, which routes all your calls and SMS (including OTPs) to the attacker's SIM. This enables complete bypass of all SMS-based two-factor authentication.

Scenario: Email: 'Jumia Order Confirmation: Your order #JMO-99821 for Samsung Galaxy A55 (₦189,000) has been placed. If you did not place this order, click CANCEL ORDER immediately at: jumia-order-cancel.com.'
OptionA: Click Cancel Order immediately — ₦189,000 is too much to lose
OptionB: Log into your Jumia account at jumia.com.ng directly to check your actual order history
OptionC: Call Jumia customer support at 01-888-8220
CorrectAnswer: Log into your Jumia account at jumia.com.ng directly to check your actual order history
Hint: Jumia's real domain is jumia.com.ng. 'jumia-order-cancel.com' is a phishing domain. The fake order creates Fear of financial loss that drives panic clicks. Your actual Jumia account on jumia.com.ng is the only source of truth for your order history.
Explanation: Fake order confirmation phishing exploits the Fear of an unauthorised charge. The specific product and amount create False Legitimacy. Logging into your real Jumia account immediately shows no such order exists.

Scenario: SMS: 'Konga Flash Sale: 80% OFF all electronics for FIRST 100 customers today only! MacBook Pro now ₦250,000. Order at konga-flash-deals.com. Offer expires at midnight. Limited units.'
OptionA: Order quickly before the units run out — 80% off a MacBook Pro is an unbeatable deal
OptionB: Check Konga's official website at konga.com directly for active promotions
OptionC: Order one and also share with family
CorrectAnswer: Check Konga's official website at konga.com directly for active promotions
Hint: A MacBook Pro at ₦250,000 (80% off its real ₦1.2M+ price) is an impossible deal. 'konga-flash-deals.com' is not Konga's domain. Flash sale scams collect payment for goods that never arrive.
Explanation: E-commerce scam sites exploit Greed and Scarcity (first 100 customers, midnight deadline). Prices that are too good to be true are the clearest red flag. Konga's legitimate domain is konga.com.

Scenario: Email: 'PayStack: Your merchant payout of ₦340,000 has been placed on hold pending account verification. Verify your account details at paystack-merchant-verify.com within 72 hours or funds will be reversed to customers.'
OptionA: Verify immediately — ₦340,000 reversal would destroy customer relationships
OptionB: Log into your Paystack dashboard directly at dashboard.paystack.com to check payout status
OptionC: Reply to the email to request more information
CorrectAnswer: Log into your Paystack dashboard directly at dashboard.paystack.com to check payout status
Hint: Paystack's official domain is paystack.com. The dashboard is at dashboard.paystack.com. 'paystack-merchant-verify.com' is a fraudulent domain. All payout and compliance information is in your actual dashboard.
Explanation: Merchant payment platform phishing exploits both Fear of financial loss and Fear of customer damage. Business owners are particularly vulnerable because payout delays have real operational consequences.
PsychPrinciple: Fear of Financial Loss + Reputational Fear

Scenario: A vendor calls: 'We supplied your company with IT equipment worth ₦4.5M in Q2. Due to our accounting migration, we lost your remittance details. Can you please provide your company's bank details and the authorising manager's name so we can reconcile our records?'
OptionA: Provide the details to help the vendor reconcile — it's your company's money too
OptionB: Tell the vendor to submit a formal written request through your procurement department
OptionC: Give the bank name but not account number
CorrectAnswer: Tell the vendor to submit a formal written request through your procurement department
Hint: Procurement reconciliation always goes through formal purchase order systems. Verbal bank detail collection over the phone is a classic payment diversion fraud (changing payment destination to attacker's account). Procurement has the original contract records.
Explanation: Payment diversion fraud is a major corporate financial crime. Attackers research real vendor relationships and call posing as that vendor. Diverting even one payment of ₦4.5M is a single successful attack. All vendor bank detail changes must go through procurement's documented verification process.

Scenario: WhatsApp: 'Hi, I need someone to help me receive a PayPal transfer and convert to Naira. I'll give you 10% commission. PayPal only works with foreign accounts so I need your ATM card number, expiry, and CVV to send the funds. Completely safe, just one transfer.'
OptionA: Help out — 10% of a PayPal transfer is easy income
OptionB: Decline firmly — this is a money mule request and providing card details is extremely dangerous
OptionC: Help but use an old card that has no money
CorrectAnswer: Decline firmly — this is a money mule request and providing card details is extremely dangerous
Hint: Your card number, expiry, and CVV are everything needed to make card-not-present purchases. This is either a money mule scheme or direct card fraud. There is no scenario where giving these details to a stranger is safe.
Explanation: This is a money mule recruitment / card fraud operation. The PayPal story is a Pretext. Your card details enable immediate online purchases. 'Using an old card' is not protection — if it has any credit or overdraft facility, it is vulnerable.

Scenario: You are walking to your car after work and notice a USB drive on the ground near your company's main entrance. It has a small sticker: 'EMPLOYEE PERFORMANCE REVIEWS 2025'.
OptionA: Pick it up and plug it into your work laptop to review the contents
OptionB: Leave it and report its location to your company's security desk
OptionC: Pick it up and plug it into your personal laptop at home
CorrectAnswer: Leave it and report its location to your company's security desk
Hint: This is a textbook USB drop attack, one of the most documented social engineering techniques. The 'Employee Reviews' label is designed to maximise Curiosity and Self-Interest. Any device is vulnerable.
Explanation: USB drop attacks at corporate entrances are highly targeted. Studies show 45-98% of dropped USB drives get plugged in (depending on the label). The 'performance reviews' label exploits Curiosity about others and Self-Interest about your own review.

Scenario: A person in a delivery uniform arrives at your ministry office: 'I have a package from the Ministry of Finance that must be signed by the Director personally. The package contains sensitive financial documents. Can I wait at his desk while you call him down? I just need to place it there before my next delivery.'
OptionA: Allow the delivery person to wait at the Director's desk — it's just waiting
OptionB: Have the delivery person wait in the reception area and call security to inform them of the visitor
OptionC: Allow them in but follow them to the desk
CorrectAnswer: Have the delivery person wait in the reception area and call security to inform them of the visitor
Hint: Allowing an unverified visitor — even a delivery person — into any private office creates a physical security risk. They could plant a hardware keylogger, listening device, or simply photograph sensitive documents on the desk.
Explanation: Physical access attacks via delivery pretexting are used for espionage and data theft. Even a brief unsupervised moment at a senior official's desk enables: hardware keylogger installation (30 seconds), document photography, USB insertion, listening device placement. Reception is the correct location for all visitors.

Scenario: Your company's reception calls: 'There's someone here claiming to be from Cisco TAC on-site support. He says he has an appointment with the IT team for the router maintenance. He doesn't have a visitor badge but says it was arranged last-minute. Should I let him up?'
OptionA: Let him come up — Cisco TAC visits happen sometimes
OptionB: Ask the reception to hold the visitor while you check with your team and verify with Cisco TAC on your official case number
OptionC: Let him come up but accompany him at all times
CorrectAnswer: Ask the reception to hold the visitor while you check with your team and verify with Cisco TAC on your official case number
Hint: All vendor on-site visits must be pre-arranged, documented, and verified against your open support cases. Cisco TAC visits are scheduled through your service contract — you will have a case number. No legitimate vendor shows up unannounced for 'maintenance'.
Explanation: Tailgating / physical social engineering via vendor impersonation. An attacker with physical access to your core networking equipment can: install hardware backdoors, copy running configurations, redirect traffic. Always verify vendor visits against official case numbers.

Scenario: A hospital colleague passes you a memory card: 'I found this SD card near the radiology department. It might have patient images on it that need to be returned. Can you check what's on it from your workstation?'
OptionA: Insert the SD card in your workstation to identify the patient data and help return it
OptionB: Hand it to your hospital's IT security department without inserting it anywhere
OptionC: Insert it in a personal tablet that isn't connected to hospital systems
CorrectAnswer: Hand it to your hospital's IT security department without inserting it anywhere
Hint: The 'found media with patient data' scenario is specifically engineered to bypass your hesitation using Duty of Care. Hospital IT can forensically examine the card safely. Personal devices are also targets.
Explanation: This Baiting attack is designed for healthcare workers: it uses your Professional Duty to protect patient data against you. SD card malware can use the host computer as a pivot into the hospital network, potentially compromising connected medical devices and radiology systems.

Scenario: In your university library, you find an A4 printout on a table: 'Free WiFi — Connect to 'Library_Free_WiFi' for unlimited access. Password: library2025'. The real library WiFi is called 'UniLibrary_Secure'.
OptionA: Connect to 'Library_Free_WiFi' — free unlimited internet is useful for research
OptionB: Stay on the official 'UniLibrary_Secure' network only
OptionC: Connect using only your phone, not your laptop
CorrectAnswer: Stay on the official 'UniLibrary_Secure' network only
Hint: This is an Evil Twin WiFi attack — a rogue access point designed to look like a free network. All your traffic on 'Library_Free_WiFi' is visible to the attacker: passwords, emails, academic portals, and banking apps.
Explanation: Evil Twin attacks set up rogue WiFi access points to intercept all traffic. On an attacker-controlled network, HTTPS can be downgraded via SSLstrip attacks on misconfigured sites. Banking apps with certificate pinning are safer, but any unencrypted traffic is fully readable.

Scenario: Email: 'Binance Nigeria: Your account shows a login from a new device in Lagos. If this was not you, immediately secure your account at: binance-ng-security.com. Enter your email, password, and 2FA code to freeze the suspicious session.'
OptionA: Secure your account immediately — a hacked Binance account means losing crypto
OptionB: Open Binance directly at binance.com and check your account security settings
OptionC: Reply to the email saying it was you
CorrectAnswer: Open Binance directly at binance.com and check your account security settings
Hint: Binance's domain is binance.com — 'binance-ng-security.com' is fraudulent. Never enter your 2FA code on a site you reached via email — doing so gives the attacker real-time access to drain your account.
Explanation: Crypto exchange phishing is the most financially devastating category — once crypto is transferred, it is irreversible. The 2FA code request is particularly dangerous: attackers use real-time relay attacks where they instantly use your credentials and 2FA code on the real Binance site.

Scenario: SMS: 'P2P ALERT: Your USDT wallet has received 500 USDT from an unknown sender. To confirm or return this transfer, verify at: crypto-verify-ng.com. Unverified transfers are frozen within 1 hour.'
OptionA: Verify quickly before the transfer is frozen
OptionB: Ignore the SMS and check your actual crypto wallet app directly
OptionC: Visit the site to return the funds — it would be wrong to keep someone else's money
CorrectAnswer: Ignore the SMS and check your actual crypto wallet app directly
Hint: Unexpected crypto transfers do not require external verification. This SMS fabricates both a reward (free USDT) and a threat (frozen transfer) simultaneously. Your wallet app shows your actual balance — no external site is needed.
Explanation: This sophisticated Smishing exploits both Greed (free USDT) and Fear (frozen funds) simultaneously. The 'unverified transfer' narrative does not exist in real blockchain systems — transactions are immutable and don't require user confirmation. The site collects your wallet seed phrase.

Scenario: Call: 'Hi, I'm Samuel from a fintech startup. We're raising capital from early investors. We've already gotten ₦50M from our seed investors. We're offering students 50% ROI in 60 days. The minimum is ₦30,000. We're on a CBN regulatory sandbox pilot so it's fully licensed.'
OptionA: Invest ₦30,000 — CBN regulatory sandbox means it's legitimate
OptionB: Research the startup on the CBN website's list of licensed fintechs and sandbox participants before any investment
OptionC: Invest a smaller amount like ₦10,000 to test the returns
CorrectAnswer: Research the startup on the CBN website's list of licensed fintechs and sandbox participants before any investment
Hint: CBN Regulatory Sandbox participants are publicly listed on the CBN website. 'Sandbox' does not mean licensed for public deposit-taking. 50% ROI in 60 days (300% annual) is mathematically impossible from legitimate business.
Explanation: Investment scams targeting students use technical-sounding legitimacy (CBN sandbox) to create False Authority. Real CBN sandbox participants are listed at cbn.gov.ng and are not authorised to take public deposits. No legitimate investment guarantees 50% in 60 days.

Scenario: You receive a Telegram message from someone with a verified-looking badge: 'Hey, I'm a security researcher. I found a zero-day in your company's crypto custody system. I'll sell you the exploit and full technical report for 0.5 BTC (about ₦35M). This is my way of responsible disclosure — I need to be compensated fairly for my work.'
OptionA: Pay the 0.5 BTC — a zero-day in your custody system is a catastrophic risk
OptionB: Report the contact to your CISO and your relevant law enforcement (EFCC cybercrime unit)
OptionC: Negotiate the price down before paying
CorrectAnswer: Report the contact to your CISO and your relevant law enforcement (EFCC cybercrime unit)
Hint: Legitimate bug bounty and responsible disclosure never involves demanding Bitcoin payment under threat. This is extortion — in Nigeria, a criminal offence under the Cybercrime Act 2015. EFCC cybercrime unit handles these cases.
Explanation: This is a crypto extortion / cyberextortion attack. 'Responsible disclosure' never involves BTC payment demands — real researchers submit to bug bounty programmes or VDPs. Paying does not guarantee the exploit is real or that it won't be sold elsewhere. This is extortion under Section 24 of the Cybercrime Act 2015.
PsychPrinciple: Fear of Catastrophic Loss + Extortion + False Research Authority

Scenario: An acquaintance you met at a party messages you: 'I've been making consistent returns on a Bitcoin investment scheme. My mentor Blockchain-Kingsley on Instagram has a ₦100,000 investment that yields ₦400,000 in 30 days. I can refer you and you both get a bonus. I've already received ₦800,000 from two previous investments.'
OptionA: Invest ₦100,000 — your acquaintance's track record is convincing
OptionB: Decline — 400% in 30 days is a Ponzi scheme regardless of who recommends it
OptionC: Invest a smaller amount to test the legitimacy
CorrectAnswer: Decline — 400% in 30 days is a Ponzi scheme regardless of who recommends it
Hint: Ponzi schemes pay early investors with later investors' money, creating real testimonials. Your acquaintance may genuinely have received ₦800,000 — which is how Ponzi schemes build credibility. 400% in 30 days is mathematically impossible from real investment.
Explanation: This is a Ponzi scheme using Social Proof (real returns paid to recruits) and Greed. The 'I've already made money' testimonial is the Ponzi mechanism — early investors receive real payments funded by new investors. When recruitment stops, the scheme collapses. Investigators, not investments, is the correct response.
PsychPrinciple: Social Proof + Greed + Ponzi Scheme Mechanics

Scenario: You receive an email from 'security@gtbank-alert.com' addressed to Students: 'Your GTBank account has been restricted after a ₦500,000 transfer attempt to an account in Kaduna was flagged. Verify identity at gtbank-secure-ng.com within 24 hours to restore access.'
OptionA: Click the verification link immediately to restore your account
OptionB: Log into your GTBank app directly to check for any restrictions
OptionC: Call GTBank customer care using the number on the back of your card
CorrectAnswer: Log into your GTBank app directly to check for any restrictions
Hint: 'gtbank-alert.com' and 'gtbank-secure-ng.com' are NOT GTBank's official domains. GTBank's real domain ends in its registered web address. Always go to your banking app first.
Explanation: This phishing attack spoofs GTBank using two lookalike domains — one for the sender and one for the verification page. The account restriction threat creates Fear. The city name adds False Legitimacy by suggesting insider knowledge. Your GTBank app is always the correct first check.

Scenario: You receive an email from 'security@accessbank-alert.com' addressed to Students: 'Your Access Bank account has been restricted after a ₦500,000 transfer attempt to an account in Benin City was flagged. Verify identity at accessbank-secure-ng.com within 24 hours to restore access.'
OptionA: Click the verification link immediately to restore your account
OptionB: Log into your Access Bank app directly to check for any restrictions
OptionC: Call Access Bank customer care using the number on the back of your card
CorrectAnswer: Log into your Access Bank app directly to check for any restrictions
Hint: 'accessbank-alert.com' and 'accessbank-secure-ng.com' are NOT Access Bank's official domains. Access Bank's real domain ends in its registered web address. Always go to your banking app first.
Explanation: This phishing attack spoofs Access Bank using two lookalike domains — one for the sender and one for the verification page. The account restriction threat creates Fear. Scenario: A call: 'Good day, I am Officer Bola from the CBN. your bank account is linked to suspected money laundering. All funds will be frozen unless you transfer to a CBN holding account. This is extremely time-sensitive. Provide your BVN and bank details to begin the clearance process immediately.'
OptionA: Cooperate with the officer — CBN has serious enforcement powers
OptionB: Ask for their badge number and tell them you will call the CBN official line independently to verify
OptionC: Provide your account number only but not your BVN
CorrectAnswer: Ask for their badge number and tell them you will call the CBN official line independently to verify
Hint: CBN and all Nigerian enforcement agencies have official contact numbers listed at their official .gov.ng websites. Legitimate officers always welcome identity verification — only fraudsters resist it.
Explanation: This Vishing attack impersonates CBN to create extreme Fear. No enforcement agency legitimately collects BVN or bank details by phone as part of an investigation — all financial freezing orders go through courts. Calling back on an independently sourced number exposes the fraud.
PsychPrinciple: Fear + Fear of Asset Seizure + Authority Impersonation

Scenario: Someone posing as a selling a product (Facebook Marketplace seller) contacts you and asks you to 'verify payment' by sharing your bank app OTP with them to 'confirm your account is real and prevent scammers'.
OptionA: Comply with the request to resolve the situation quickly
OptionB: Refuse — OTPs are one-time transaction authorisation codes that must never be shared with anyone
OptionC: Partially comply by providing less sensitive information
CorrectAnswer: Refuse — OTPs are one-time transaction authorisation codes that must never be shared with anyone
Hint: OTPs (One-Time Passwords) are transaction authorisation codes. Sharing an OTP gives the recipient full authorisation to complete whatever transaction triggered it — typically draining your account.
Explanation: This Pretexting exploits marketplace trust. The 'anti-scam verification' story is designed to make sharing an OTP feel protective. OTPs authorise transactions — sharing one completes the attacker's fraudulent transfer.

Scenario: Someone posing as a delivering a package requiring payment (Delivery company) contacts you and asks for your debit card details over the phone to 'process a ₦1,500 customs clearance fee' before delivery.
OptionA: Comply with the request to resolve the situation quickly
OptionB: Refuse and request payment through a verifiable official payment link from the delivery company's official website
OptionC: Partially comply by providing less sensitive information
CorrectAnswer: Refuse and request payment through a verifiable official payment link from the delivery company's official website
Hint: Legitimate delivery companies in Nigeria use POS terminals on delivery or direct bank transfer with verified account numbers — not card detail collection by phone.
Explanation: Delivery fee pretexting is common. Card details collected by phone enable card-not-present fraud globally. ₦1,500 is a deliberately small amount to avoid triggering suspicion.

Scenario: Someone posing as a following up on an account complaint (Bank customer service) contacts you and calls you back about a complaint you never filed, and asks for your full card details to 'process the resolution and reverse the erroneous charge'.
OptionA: Comply with the request to resolve the situation quickly
OptionB: Hang up and call your bank directly using the number on the back of your card to check if any complaint or charge exists
OptionC: Partially comply by providing less sensitive information
CorrectAnswer: Hang up and call your bank directly using the number on the back of your card to check if any complaint or charge exists
Hint: If you never filed a complaint, this is a cold call fishing expedition. Even if you had a real issue, your bank's call centre resolves it by verifying you — not by collecting your full card number.
Explanation: The 'complaint follow-up' Pretext creates False Reciprocity — the bank is 'helping you', so you should cooperate. Your full card number plus CVV enables card-not-present fraud. Real bank resolutions are initiated by the customer, not unsolicited calls.

Scenario: Someone posing as a on WhatsApp (Crypto trading group admin) contacts you and shares consistent screenshots of 3x returns and offers to manage your funds personally. They ask for your exchange API keys with withdrawal permissions 'so the bot can trade on your behalf automatically'.
OptionA: Comply with the request to resolve the situation quickly
OptionB: Refuse — API keys with withdrawal permissions give full control of your exchange account to whoever holds them
OptionC: Partially comply by providing less sensitive information
CorrectAnswer: Refuse — API keys with withdrawal permissions give full control of your exchange account to whoever holds them
Hint: Exchange API keys with withdrawal permissions are equivalent to handing over your full account access. Trading bots only need read and trade permissions — never withdrawal permissions.
Explanation: Crypto trading group scams use Social Proof (screenshots of returns) and Greed. API keys with withdrawal permissions enable complete account draining with no recourse. Legitimate managed trading services use segregated custodial accounts, not direct API access.

Scenario: Someone posing as a via student WhatsApp group (University course lecturer) contacts you and asks students to send their student ID and portal login credentials to a personal email address for 'grading system migration purposes'.
OptionA: Comply with the request to resolve the situation quickly
OptionB: Refuse and report the message to the university's IT department — lecturers never need students' portal passwords
OptionC: Partially comply by providing less sensitive information
CorrectAnswer: Refuse and report the message to the university's IT department — lecturers never need students' portal passwords
Hint: University portal credentials are personal and non-transferable. Lecturers manage grades through their own administrative accounts — they never need students' passwords.
Explanation: Academic authority Pretexting exploits students' compliance with lecturer instructions. Portal credentials give access to your academic records, personal data, and can be used to fraudulently alter grades or access financial aid information.

Scenario: You encounter a QR code sticker on an ATM labelled 'FREE BONUS CARD — SPECIAL PROMOTION — SCAN QR FOR ₦10,000 CREDIT' in a ATM machine.
OptionA: Interact with the device/content out of curiosity or helpfulness
OptionB: Report the sticker to the bank's security and do NOT scan the QR code
OptionC: Interact briefly to verify it is safe before committing
CorrectAnswer: Report the sticker to the bank's security and do NOT scan the QR code
Hint: QR codes on or near ATMs are a known attack vector. They can direct to phishing sites that harvest card details or install malware on your phone.
Explanation: ATM QR code baiting is documented in Nigeria and globally. The QR leads to a credential-harvesting page styled to look like your bank's internet banking login.

Scenario: You encounter a USB charging port at a public charging station labelled 'FREE PHONE CHARGING STATION' in a charging station in a public space.
OptionA: Interact with the device/content out of curiosity or helpfulness
OptionB: Use your own power adapter plugged into the AC socket, or decline to charge
OptionC: Interact briefly to verify it is safe before committing
CorrectAnswer: Use your own power adapter plugged into the AC socket, or decline to charge
Hint: Public USB charging ports can deliver data as well as power — a technique called 'juice jacking'. Only AC outlets with your own adapter are completely safe.
Explanation: Juice jacking attacks use modified USB charging ports to install malware or extract data while your phone charges. Public USB ports in malls, airports, and transport hubs have been documented as attack vectors.

Scenario: You encounter a laptop left open and unlocked in the conference room labelled 'BOARD STRATEGY PRESENTATION — Q4 2025 — DO NOT SHARE' in a conference room after a meeting.
OptionA: Interact with the device/content out of curiosity or helpfulness
OptionB: Lock the screen, close the laptop, and report it to IT security
OptionC: Interact briefly to verify it is safe before committing
CorrectAnswer: Lock the screen, close the laptop, and report it to IT security
Hint: An unattended, unlocked device is an open door for data theft, malware installation, or simple credential theft. Even a 30-second window is enough for an attacker.
Explanation: Unattended device baiting exploits Curiosity and opportunity. 30 seconds at an unlocked computer is enough to install a Remote Access Trojan, copy sensitive files, or plant a hardware keylogger.

Hint: Found devices may be planted baiting tools or may contain privacy-protected personal data. Browsing someone's phone — even if found — may violate privacy laws.

Hint: Found devices may be planted baiting tools or may contain privacy-protected personal data. Browsing someone's phone — even if found — may violate privacy laws.
Explanation: Found device baiting plants a phone preloaded with malware that activates when browsed. Even genuine found phones should be handed to management without browsing, for both legal and security reasons.

Scenario: You encounter a popup advertisement on a shopping website labelled 'CLICK HERE — YOU ARE TODAY'S LUCKY VISITOR! CLAIM ₦50,000 GIFT CARD' in a e-commerce website.
OptionA: Interact with the device/content out of curiosity or helpfulness
OptionB: Close the popup without clicking and run your browser's malware scanner
OptionC: Interact briefly to verify it is safe before committing
CorrectAnswer: Close the popup without clicking and run your browser's malware scanner
Hint: Browser popup ads claiming you are a 'lucky visitor' are either adware/malware delivery mechanisms or credential-harvesting portals.
Explanation: Malvertising (malicious advertising) delivers malware through legitimate-looking browser popups. Clicking the popup can: install adware, redirect your browser, or display a fake prize collection page.
